old_claim.note: Internet Protocols (TCP/IP, ARPANET) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because standardized ADSL is a physical-layer copper access technology; broadband Internet demand drove deployment, but TCP/IP is not required for the ADSL transceiver interface itself.

source_shape.source_locator: ITU-T G.992.1 describes ADSL transceivers on a metallic twisted pair, bearer channels, and physical-layer characteristics between network-operator and customer equipment.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The standard defines the ADSL copper-loop physical interface rather than an Internet protocol dependency.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removing TCP/IP as a direct prerequisite because Internet service is a major application and adoption driver, not a prerequisite for the scoped DSL transceiver technology.

ontology_before: The graph made standardized DSL depend directly on Internet protocols by broad broadband-access inference.
ontology_after: The graph models DSL as a copper access/transceiver technology; Internet protocols remain downstream service context rather than a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: direct digital_subscriber_line_dsl -> internet_protocols_tcp_ip_arpanet edge removed.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to consumer DSL Internet service bundles rather than the DSL access technology.
A stronger source showed ADSL transceivers require TCP/IP rather than carrying multiple bearer/service types.
short_reason: TCP/IP is service context, not a DSL transceiver prerequisite.
